## VI. What This Map Is Good For

::: {.aside .meta-note}
**META:** Portrait-specific closing. Brief. Emphasizes the portrait framework's specific value (crux-tracing and mapping belief profiles) and references Section V (Where I Stand) as the next beat.
:::

This portrait framework is useful for a specific thing: locating where the intellectual fault lines actually lie between the groups. If you come away knowing that Christiano and Yudkowsky aren't disagreeing about how bad a misaligned superintelligence would be, but about whether current AI behavior is relevant evidence and whether warning shots are actionable — the map worked. If you can see that Bengio and Yudkowsky share a threat model almost entirely and diverge only on whether architectural choices can prevent it — the map worked. Surfacing these underlying cruxes is not the same as resolving them, but it is a necessary precondition for productive debate.

What the map is not useful for: telling you who's right. That's a different project. Some cruxes (especially A and C) are in principle resolvable by empirical evidence that could arrive in the next few years. Others — especially D and the deeper parts of B — are prior-dependent in ways that may not converge even with shared observations. The epistemological stance cut (§IV) exists precisely to name this: some of these conversations are structurally unresolvable under current conditions because the thinkers do not share an evidentiary standard.

An honest caveat: this entire mapping project takes a position. It assumes that the debate is conducted in good faith, that crux-identification is the productive move, and that the relevant disagreements are tractable to this kind of decomposition. That's a decomposition-first stance (Axis 4, Gradualist Forecaster territory) — which means a committed prior-dominant thinker might find the whole framing beside the point. If you've read this far and feel like it missed something essential about the structure of the problem, that disagreement is also worth naming explicitly.

The final utility of this mapping project is found in the transition space of §IV. The map exists to locate the crux. The next time you find yourself in a deadlocked conversation about AI risk, stop asking for their p(doom). Ask them which crux they are treating as settled — and, if you are ready to map your own coordinates, see where they land in the personal alignment of §V.

---

## Appendix / Notes

::: {.aside .meta-note}
**META:** Structural scratchpad — open questions, design decisions under consideration, things to check before publishing. Items here are not blocking; they're reminders.
:::

- *[Consider: comparison heatmap across archetypes × cruxes — visual version of the crux table in §IV]*
- *[Pressure-test: are all seven archetypes genuinely distinct? Particular question: is Conditional Doomer doing different work than Structural Doomer enough to warrant two portraits?]*

---

## Sources

::: {.aside .meta-note}
**META:** Organized by archetype. When adding inline citations to prose, add the source here too. Items marked `[CITATION: ...]` need a URL that hasn't been confirmed yet. Verify all links before publish.
:::

**#1 — Hard Doomer**

- Yudkowsky, ["AGI Ruin: A List of Lethalities"](https://www.lesswrong.com/posts/uMQ3cqWDPHhjtiesc/agi-ruin-a-list-of-lethalities) (LessWrong, Apr 2022)
- Yudkowsky, ["Pausing AI Developments Isn't Enough. We Need to Shut It All Down"](https://time.com/6266923/ai-eliezer-yudkowsky-open-letter-not-enough/) (TIME, Mar 2023)
- MIRI, ["2024 Mission and Strategy Update"](https://www.lesswrong.com/posts/xofpLpFjkMFgYX6JC/miri-2024-mission-and-strategy-update) (LessWrong, Jan 2024)

**#2 — Structural Doomer**

- Bostrom, *[Superintelligence](https://global.oup.com/academic/product/superintelligence-9780198739838)* (Oxford, 2014)
- Bostrom, *[Deep Utopia](https://www.amazon.com/Deep-Utopia-Meaning-Solved-World/dp/1646871952)* (2024) — position shift
- Carlsmith, [*Is Power-Seeking AI an Existential Risk?*](https://arxiv.org/abs/2206.13353) (arXiv, 2022)

**#3 — Conditional Doomer / Risk Realist**

- Christiano, ["My views on 'doom'"](https://www.alignmentforum.org/posts/a392MCzsGXAZP88Sq/my-views-on-doom) (Alignment Forum, Apr 2023)
- Amodei, 25% p(doom) — [Axios interview](https://www.axios.com/2025/09/17/anthropic-dario-amodei-p-doom-25-percent) (Sep 2025)

**#4 — Multipolar / Governance Realist**

- Critch & Krueger, [ARCHES](https://arxiv.org/abs/2006.04948) (arXiv, 2020)
- Toner, [Georgetown CSET](https://cset.georgetown.edu)
- Russell, *[Human Compatible](https://www.amazon.com/Human-Compatible-Artificial-Intelligence-Problem/dp/0525558616)* (Viking, 2019)
- Ord, *[The Precipice](https://theprecipice.com/)* (Hachette, 2020)
- *[Critch p(doom) ~85% — stated across multiple public talks and interviews 2022–2024; no single canonical URL. The ARCHES paper (above) contains the relevant formal work.]*

**#5 — Safe-by-Design Alarmist**

- Bengio et al., ["Managing Extreme AI Risks Amid Rapid Progress"](https://www.science.org/doi/10.1126/science.adn0117) (*Science*, May 2024)
- [International AI Safety Report](https://www.gov.uk/government/publications/international-ai-safety-report-2025) (Jan 2025)
- Russell & Norvig, [CIRL](https://arxiv.org/abs/1606.03137) (arXiv, 2016)
- Russell, *[Human Compatible](https://www.amazon.com/Human-Compatible-Artificial-Intelligence-Problem/dp/0525558616)* (Viking, 2019)

**#6 — Capability / Paradigm Skeptic**

- LeCun, ["A Path Towards Autonomous Machine Intelligence"](https://openreview.net/pdf?id=BZ5a1r-kVsf) (2022)
- LeCun, "AI isn't even as smart as dogs or cats" (Viva Tech, Paris, Jun 2023 — direct link unavailable; quote reported in multiple outlets)
- Chollet, [ARC Prize / ARC-AGI](https://arcprize.org)

**#7 — Gradualist Forecaster**

- Grace et al., ["Thousands of AI Authors on the Future of AI"](https://arxiv.org/abs/2401.02843) (arXiv:2401.02843, Jan 2024)
